pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database (cameochemicals.noaa.gov) and chemical reference databases
Web search of vapor pressure data: Acetone 181.7-187.5 mmHg at 20°C (highest), Methanol 94-97 mmHg, Ethyl Acetate 73 mmHg, Toluene 21-28 mmHg
Vapor pressure rankings: Acetone (185 mmHg) > Methanol (96 mmHg) > Ethyl Acetate (73 mmHg) > Toluene (25 mmHg)
Web search results: Acetone vapor pressure at 20°C is 181.7-185 mmHg from Wikipedia, homework.study.com, and Shell technical datasheet (25 kPa ≈ 187.5 mmHg)
Web search results: Acetone boiling point is 133°F (56.1°C) from CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database
Web search results: CDC NIOSH data shows 21 mmHg at 23.3°C, with range of 21-28 mmHg at 20-25°C depending on temperature
Web search results: Toluene boiling point is 231.1°F (110.6°C) from CAMEO Chemicals NOAA database
Web search results: Methanol vapor pressure at 20°C is 94-97 mmHg from Wikipedia, Engineering Toolbox, and chemical databases
Web search results: Methanol boiling point is 64.6°C (147°F) from multiple sources including CK-12 Foundation and chemical references
Web search results: Ethyl Acetate vapor pressure at 20°C is 73 mmHg from Sigma-Aldrich, CDC NIOSH, and safety data sheets
Web search results: Ethyl Acetate boiling point is 77°C from NIST, Sigma-Aldrich, and CDC NIOSH data

privileged_info_summary: All four chemicals (Acetone, Toluene, Methanol, Ethyl Acetate) are real industrial solvents with confirmed CAS numbers. The CAMEO Chemicals database (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/) is a real NOAA public resource. Physical property data is verified: Acetone has the highest vapor pressure (181.7-187.5 mmHg at 20°C), making it the greatest inhalation hazard. The ranking by vapor pressure is Acetone > Methanol > Ethyl Acetate > Toluene, which matches the metadata claim. Boiling points are Acetone (56.1°C), Methanol (64.6°C), Ethyl Acetate (77°C), Toluene (110.6°C). All property ranges in metadata align with published chemical reference data.
